videos.transcript: Ladies and gentlemen, welcome to Riga, the captivating capital city of Latvia. In this comprehensive travel guide, I'll take you on a journey through the best attractions. How to get in from the airport, how to get around the city, where to eat, and how to ensure you make the most of your visit to this charming Baltic city. Riga is a city that's full of medieval charm and rich history. But it definitely helps to know your way around the place, so let's take a look at a map. You'll notice that most of the attractions you'll be interested in are located right here in the Old Town, and if you're staying around here, all the major attractions are within a short walking distance. So how do you get to and from the airport? Most likely, you'll arrive here at Riga International Airport. You can take a taxi if you want to, but the easiest and cheapest way is to actually use the bus. Specifically bus number 22, which you can catch from just outside the airport terminal. To buy tickets, find this machine located outside and select your language. My advice to you is to buy an all-inclusive travel pass for the number of days that you're planning to be here. Once you pay for it, it'll spit you out this orange ticket. When you board any mode of transport, all you need to do; is simply validate it with one of the validators and away you go. It's literally that simple. And the same method can be used for the trams. So now that you're here, let's visit the most visited and iconic attraction here in the city of Riga. The House of the Blackheads. This absolutely stunning building, complete with an ornate anatomical clock at the front, is Riga's most iconic landmark and possibly the symbol of the city itself. It's definitely an attraction where you can take lots of iconic photos and videos. It's called ‘The House of the Blackheads’ due to the Brotherhood of followers of St. Maurice, who happens to be a black Egyptian. Hence the name ‘Blackheads’. Once you've bought your ticket, you'll notice that it's an equally grand building inside as it is outside. The most notable feature is this lovely ballroom, complete with ornate decorations, the blackhead coat of arms, and these crystal chandeliers. It's also a lovely place to practice your ballroom dancing if you so feel like it. It's very, very beautiful inside, and it banks onto a balcony that overlooks the Old Town and City Hall. You'll encounter other quaint little rooms that are very very nicely decorated, and you can learn all about the history of the blackhead order and the timeline of construction of this building. There's definitely lots of cool things to see and do in the House of the Blackheads and it's definitely worth the price of admission. Even if you don't want to go inside, that's okay, it still looks pretty awesome from out here at Town Square. Across from Town Square you'll find Riga Town Hall. Beautiful building, but you can't go inside and urgh … what an appalling conveyance that is! Around the corner you'll find St Peter's Church, one of the more important churches here in the city of Riga. It's beautifully decorated inside, very much clad with brick, and is a lovely serene Church to walk around. The best part of St Peters’ is if you go up the bell tower to take in these lovely views across the entire city of Riga. You can see all the medieval streets from up high, beautiful views of the river, and it's a 360° view across the entire town, especially if it's a nice day. Around the corner from that, you'll find St John's Church. With its beautiful ceiling detail, cool looking mirror. Hello there! And overall, it is free to go inside - but if you are taking photos and videos they highly recommend that you make a donation, which I gladly did. There are many other churches around the Old Town of Riga that you can visit, and yes, you can go inside them if you want to. Most of them are indeed free. But possibly the most important one that charges an entry fee is this: Riga Cathedral. One of the main buildings that you can see on the Riga skyline. Outside, you'll find this cool Riga sign, which makes for some nice photo and video opportunities. There's some interesting looking statues out here. Armadillos! And on the inside, it's a very Grand Cathedral indeed. As always with any Cathedral there's a lot of very interesting Church stuff on display. But the most surprising thing about Riga Cathedral is going around the cloisters AKA where the monks kind of hang out. These cloisters are incredibly ancient, but they're really cool to explore. Some parts of it are very beautifully decorated. Some parts of it are incredibly creepy. But overall, you'll have a good time here. But if churches isn't your thing, you'll have a whale of a time just walking the streets. You'll marvel at the architecture, it's very medieval and it feels like you step back into the Middle Ages. On a nice day like today, you'll enjoy the atmosphere, walking around medieval buildings, that happen to have modern shops inside of them. And there are plenty of places to eat particularly the eateries around Riga Cathedral. And as we're talking about food; Latvian Cuisine can be found virtually anywhere. And there's no corner of the city where you can't find traditional food, in medieval style restaurants. The prices are actually quite reasonable, beer is cheap, but the most famous alcoholic drink that Riga is known for is this; Black Balsam. It's kind of an aniseed liquer, and to be honest it's really not my thing. But try it you might like it?! and there's that damn appalling conveyance again! Who invented that? Whilst wandering around, you'll eventually come across the north part of the city, and specifically to this building right here: Riga Castle. Currently a government building, whilst there is a museum inside that you can access - you can't actually access where the government does its business. This part is heavily guarded and I wouldn't mess with these guys if I were you. Let's venture now to the south side of the city, where you'll find the main train station. So if you are arriving via train, this is where you'll come into the city, and you can get to pretty much anywhere else in Latvia from here. But it's also right next to a giant shopping centre. So if you do want to go shopping in more modern shops, this is the place to go. And it's not much different to any other western shopping centre that you'll come across. But for a more authentic shopping experience around the corner you'll find Riga Central Market. It's a big giant marketplace that's all the locals seem to shop at. If I'm being brutally honest (and in my opinion) it's no place for a tourist. As quite a lot of these things you really can't take home with you. If you're looking for something a little bit quieter, Riga has several park spaces just outside the Old Town. So if you want to take it easy in the sunshine, surrounded by trees and grass, ride a boat along the river whilst enjoying the fountains and some lovely scenery, this is the part of the city for you. While you're enjoying sauntering through the parks, you'll eventually come across the National Opera House, which is the opera venue here in the city of Riga. If you wanted to catch a ballet or an opera show, this is the venue for you. Not too far away, you'll find the Freedom Monument, which is the National Monument here in Latvia. You'll find this slap bank in the middle of the road, and it separates the Old Town from the New Town. One of the cool things about these park spaces in the new town is that not only does everyone enjoy them, but everyone has access to this rather cool Outdoor Art Gallery. I think this is an incredibly good idea because it introduces people who otherwise wouldn't be interested, in art. These are some of the most famous paintings in the Prado Museum; absolutely anybody can enjoy them. And it's completely free to explore! It's definitely a nice relaxing atmosphere in the park spaces of Riga, especially if it's a nice day. You'll eventually come across the Powder Tower, which is currently home to the Latvian War Museum. It's free to go inside, and believe it or not, it's an interesting look at Latvia's military history, from the ancient, to the modern, and that guy, oh dear. When you climb the Round Tower, it has all of these exhibits, so it's definitely worth about 30 minutes of your time to explore. Then you'll come across the most iconic museum in the entire city: The Latvian National Museum of Art. When you go inside you'll be met with this grand staircase, and lovely views from the top floor. Some of the art in here is incredibly modern, it's definitely a more modern museum with incredibly cooky floors. But the paintings are hmmm interesting?! Overall it's an excellent museum and if you wanted something a little bit more substantial than a free Outdoor Art Gallery in a park, this is definitely the art gallery for you. On this side of the new town. you'll also find the ex KGB headquarters, which is now home to a KGB Museum. You'll come across many other churches, but the two most notable ones is this yellow one which is the Alexander Nevsky Church - not to be confused with the Alexander Nevski Cathedral in Tallinn, those are two different places. And the other main Church down the street, the one with the gold domes, is the Riga Nativity of Christ Cathedral. Both of these are Russian Orthodox Church, that’s that's completely decked out in gold on the inside, and they're both definitely worth a look even if you're not religious, mainly because it's free. Other attractions which are outside of the city limits, includes visiting the football stadium: home to Riga Football Club. Across the river you'll find the National Library, just in case you want to read some books in Latvian. On the south side right here is the Latvian Academy of Sciences. A very very impressive Stalin-esque building, where you can take the elevator to the 17th floor to its observation deck, for some lovely views around the city. For some absolutely stunning views; I recommend visiting the Riga TV and radio tower. But only on a clear day, because otherwise you'll be able to see nothing - like this. Overall, if you want something a little bit different and you're trying to visit all the Baltic countries, Riga is certainly worth a day or two to explore. Due to its mediaeval charm, quirky streets and historical attractions. Okay Ninh, I'm sold. What do I need to do? Well, you need to come here to Riga. As mentioned earlier in the video, to get to and from the airport, you can take a taxi. But the easiest way is to take bus number 22 from the airport, and it will drop you off just outside the House of the Blackheads. Buy yourself an all-inclusive travel pass, and you'll be absolutely fine. This allows you access to the buses and the trams, and you literally just tap your ticket to a validator in order to use it. It's literally that simple. Please note that your ticket starts from the day of validation, not the time, so if you activate your ticket at 11:55 p.m., you have effectively wasted an entire day's worth of travel. Ticket machines can be found at most major stops. The attractions are cheap enough, and most of the attractions you see in this video are actually free. But the cost of food and drink can quickly add up, so consider mixing eating out with trips to the supermarket. If you're looking for a place to stay, please stay inside the Old Town, as most of the things you want to see are within walking distance from here. I made the mistake of staying across the river from the Old Town. And whilst the hotel was very nice, it was a waste of time to commute by bus or tram to the Old Town of Riga every single day. Is there anything else I need to know? Yes, Riga's traffic is notoriously bad. Especially the roads that go into and from the Old Town. Also watch out for the cars on crossings as they don't exactly slow down or stop. Quite a lot of the city seems to be under construction and it can be a bit of a mess to walk around, so please bear that in mind. Free public toilets are rare, and with most of the public facilities here in Riga - you have to pay money to enter, so please carry some pocket change with you. If you do happen to find a free of charge toilet to use, it won't be in the nicest condition. Latvian people in general are all right, but some of them can be quite abrupt and impatient towards tourists. Don't take it personally, that's just how it is over here. If you have enjoyed this episode, please be sure to like, share and subscribe. Comment on that comment section below, and if you've got any other suggestions for bucket list ideas, you know what to do. If I get enough suggestions I'll make a video about it. But guys thanks very much for watching, and we'll see you in the next episode. Ninh Ly - www.ninh.co.uk - @NinhLyUK

videos.transcript: Oh, look at the little dog! Want to visit one of Europe's hidden gems? This city isn't usually on people's travel bucketlists. I'm in Riga, the capital of Latvia! Admittedly I don't really know that much about the city. So I'm excited to see how much I can learn and explore in just one day! I'm gonna be checking out some beautiful architecture, learning about the city and its history and trying some traditional Latvian cuisine. So let's get going! First stop of the day: breakfast! Riga has loads of traditional, small pastry shops. Everything just looked so good that I couldn't help myself and will trying loads. And cottage cheese seems to be a big thing here. I keep seeing cottage cheese pastries everywhere. So I definitely want to try these. Made a friend. Here you go. Oh yeah, that's really good. Not too sweet, a little bit savoury. A good start to the day! Okay, now I'm all fueled up for the day. Let's do some sightseeing! My favourite way to explore a new city is just to walk around, get a little bit lost. And that really helps me to get a feel for the place. I'm starting off in the medieval old town, which is a UNESCO world heritage site. The House of the Blackheads is one of THE main attractions here in Riga. It was built way back in the 14th century for the Brotherhood of Blackheads, which was a guild for unmarried merchants. The original building was bombed by the Germans in WW II and the remains were demolished by the Soviets in 1948. But it was fully restored in the late 90s. And this also happens to be the spot where the first ever Christmas tree was decorated. There's so much amazing architecture in the city. There are hundreds of Art Noveau buildings that were built during the 19th and 20th centuries. Now I want to see what the city's skyline looks like. I'm going to head to the top of Hotel Latvia. Because I've heard that I can get an amazing view of the whole city. The best thing about this view is that it's entirely free of charge. You can see the whole city skyline from up here! Time for some greenery. It's early afternoon now – the perfect time to hang out in Riga's Bastion Hill park. It's super beautiful – so quiet and peaceful, with lots of locals and tourists alike just hanging out. This huge landmark right in the middle of the park is Riga's freedom monument. It's dedicated to the national heroes who fought for the country's freedom between 1918 and 1920. Freedom is an important topic here in Latvia, a country that's been occupied for much its existence. To find out more about the city's history, I'm heading to the newly reopened Museum of the Occupation of Latvia. Why is freedom such an important topic for Latvians? I think you appreciate freedom more when you have lost it. Latvia gained it after World War I. There was the indepence war with lot of sacrifice. And then Latvia lost independence after 22 years. And the we gained it again in 1991. And you don't take it for granted once you lose it." Latvia suffered under Soviet rule for 50 years, during which people lived under constant surveillance and weren't even allowed to speak their own language. There were mass deportations during this period and thousands of people were killed. Because independence was only regained fairly recently, many Latvians have a personal connection to the fight for freedom. The biggest demonstration was in 1989 on August 23. A human chain called 'The Baltic Way'. Almost 2 million people joined hands from Tallinn this highway through Riga to Vilnius, the capital of Lithuania, almost 700 kilometers. So I was standing in that chain. It was just for very brief moments. But that feeling that you are united in 3 countries and you are all standing for the same cause in solidarity. And what's wonderful: this chain idea has been exported. In Hongkong 2016 there was this chain, in Minsk, Belarus, in many other places. And they have told us: We copied your idea. So I'm happy that from the small Baltic countries we could export this way of a non-violent resistance. Before my day here comes to a close, I have one more stop. Now that we've seen a lot of the city and learned about its history, it's time to try some more local food! This market is considered one of, if not THE biggest food market, in the whole of Europe! There's so much on offer here, from fresh fruits and vegetables, to a whole array of meat and fish and small places to grab some plates. Since Riga is so close to the sea, fish is very traditional here. So that's what I'm gonna try for dinner! Alright! This is the famous black balsam drink. It's essentially a national drink here in Latvia. But it's 45 percent, so I'm expecting it to be pretty strong. Cheers. It's actually nice. But pretty strong. It tastes like aniseed. Ok let's eat something before I get too drunk. I'm trying salmon, herring and anchovies. Got beetroot here. It's wow – supergood! Really fresh. Well, that's the end of my day in Riga! I've had such a great time here just walking around, learning about the city and exploring. And the people here are superfriendly. It's also cheaper than in lot of other European cities. Have I missed anything important? Let me know what your travel tips for Riga are – in the comments. And for now: Cheers!

videos.transcript: Hi, I am Martijn and your guide name Riga. I spent four Incredible Days in the capital of Latvia... ...and I can't wait to show you why this city should be on your travel list. From its stunning artn architecture... ...to the charm of its Cobblestone Old Town... Riga is full of surprises. Let me take you on a journey through this Hidden Gem of Europe with the best things you can do and see. The city is served by Riga International Airport and it's located 11 km outside the city center. To travel to the city you can use bus 22 and the bus stop is just outside the terminal. A ticket will set you back around €2 for 30 to 35 minutes ride and you can also buy a public transportation card for more days. Taxis are more expensive between €20 and €25. Let's start a Riga travel guide in the old town. it's well known for its medieval architecture, cobblestones and historic buildings. Picture centuries old buildings like the towering St Peter's Church and the grand Riga Cathedral framing your stroll. The Riga Cathedral also known as the Dome cathedral stands as the spiritual and cultural center of the city . This 13th century marvel boasts gothic design and houses one of the largest pipe organs in Europe. Making it a venue for world class music performances. Another historic building is the St Peter's Church. Climb to his observation platform for sweeping views of Riga'a Skyline. Where you will see that the old and modern blending seamlessly. Inside the church reveals beautiful stained glass windows and an intriguing history shaped by wars and reconstruction. A ticket will set you back €9 and I'm glad to say that there is a lift. This is the oldest residential complex in the city the Three Brothers. Three medieval houses in different architectural styles dating back to the 15th to the 17th century. It offers a glimpse into the city's rich history... ...and you can take a look inside. The main Square in the heart of the old town is the Dome Square. This iconic square is where history and and daily life converge. With festivals, markets and street performers bringing it to life. In a moment I show you this local market and some of the best museums in town but first... ...an architectural gem is the "House of the Blackheads". Originally built in the 14th century for a society of unmarried merchants. It's complete renovated in the 20th century after it was destroyed during World War II. Its decorated facade with detailed sculptures and celestial designs is truly stunning. Inside you can learn more about the history through artifacts, documents and displays. There's also the restored Grand Ballroom a highlight of the house showcasing a beautiful decor and used for concerts, events and special occasions. One of the largest and most vibrant markets in Europe is the Riga Market. It's housed in a former German Zeppelin building and pavilion. Inside you can buy souvenirs, foods clothes, and also flowers. It's a perfect place to immerse yourself in the local culture. In a moment more about an Art Nouveau District... ...and an old castle... ....but first the Museum of the Occupation of Latvia. Dedicated to the period of occupation by the Soviet Union and Nazi Germany between 1940 and 1991. Exhibits includes artifacts, photos and personal stories highlighting the struggles and resilience of Latvian people. It serves as a reminder of the importance of preserving freedom and national identity. A symbol for Latvia's Independence is the Freedom Monument. It was built in 1935 to honor those who fought for freedom. It shows a women holding free stars meaning Unity, Independence and freedom. And then an old castle which now serves as the official residence of the president of Latvia, the Riga Castle. It dates back to the 14th century and was built as a fortress to defense. And sometimes you can visit the castle when there is a tour. In a moment I show you this beautiful park, wooden houses and one of the best museums in town but first... ...Riga houses one of the largest collections of Art Nouveau architecture in the whole world... ...especially in this street Alberta. These structures are from the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th. It emerged fueled by economic growth and creative freedom. Today it showcases the city's Rich architectural history. There is also an Art Nouveau Museum which is situated in the apartment of the Latvian architect Konstantīns Pēkšēns... ...one of the key figures in Riga's Art Nouveau movement. It offers an experience with authentic interiors, period furniture and exhibitions that showcase the elegance and innovation of Art Nouveau design. One of the most visited museums in the city is the Latvian Museum of Art. It is the most significant depository in a nation for works of art. Reopened in 2016 the building itself has been upgraded modernized but its majestic beauty from a longtime era is preserved. A bur perfect place to chill and relax is the "Bastejkalna Park". It's located in the heart of the city center with canals, bridges, flowers and a lot of green spaces. In the city you can also find beautiful old wooden houses for example in this quarter. Dating back to the 19th century and it's not a top listed attraction to go but if you have some time left just walk around over here.... ...and on some days there are local markets. The total opposite of the old town is the National Library of Latvia also known as the castle of light. This new library building was opened in 2014 with collections of books, manuscripts and multimedia resources. The library serves as a center for learning, research and cultural exchange. And on the highest floor you will find this great viewing point. What is the best time for a visit I think the summer period June, July and August. You can enjoy the warmer weather there are more outdoor activities and of course the days are longer. Please give a thumbs up thank you so much when you like this Riga travel guide... ....and if you don't want to miss new episodes just subscribe on my channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view on the whole world.

A UNESCO world heritage site, Ria's Oldtown is a maze of cobblestone streets and stunning architecture that tells the story of centuries past. Ria's most iconic landmark is the House of the Blackheads. Built in 1334, this stunning Gothic building once served as the headquarters for the Brotherhood of Blackheads, a guild of influential merchants and ship owners. Heavily damaged during World War II with only its foundation surviving, the House of the Blackheads was meticulously constructed in 1990s using historic blueprints and photographs. Just a short walk away is Viv Lum's one of Ria's most lively squares. Surrounded by colorful historic buildings, this square is a popular spot for both locals and tourists to relax and enjoy the vibrant atmosphere. In the summer, it's filled with outdoors cafes and street performers, making it the perfect place to soak in the charm of the old town. If you are a fan of ballet performances or simply appreciate stunning architecture, the Hopper House in Ria's Oldtown is a mustsea landmark. Next, we visit the cat house, a great example of art no architecture, famous for his true black cat sculptures on the rooftop. Built in 1909 by a wealthy Latian merchant who was excluded from the city's great guild, the cat's tails are deliberately a turn towards the guild headquarters as a sign of defiance. Others remarkable examples of Arnova buildings in the old town are the ones located in Skoono Street 1214 and the neighbors boutique hotel at 2527 Yanuella Street. Additionally on Smilshua, the buildings at number two and 8 stand out for their decorative elements with the latter currently housing the Swedes embassy. Our next stop is Ria Cathedral, the largest medieval church in the Baltics. Dating back to 1211, this cathedral is a masterpiece of Roman-esque Gothic and Barack architecture. Inside you'll find one of the world's most renowned pipe organ for its rich and powerful sound. In front of Ria Cathedral stands the RIA Art Museum, an impressive building that showcases a diverse collection of Latvian and international art. Continuing our exploration, we arrive at the Free Brothers, the oldest residential buildings in Ria. Each house reflects a different architectural style. The oldest from the late 15th century has Gothic features. The middle house from the 1640s shows Manor's design and the youngest built in the late 17th century displays early Barack style. Nearby is the Swedish gate, the last remaining gate of Ria's old city walls. Built in 1698 during the Swedish occupation, it was part of the city's fortifications and served as a checkpoint for goods entering Ria. Last but not least, one of the most striking features of Ria's old town is St. Peter's Church with its towering spire that dominates the skyline. Originally built in the 13th century, this Gothic church offers stunning panoramic views of the city from its tower. A visit to the top is well worth it for the breathtaking scenery [Music] alone. Ria's Art Nova District located 20 minutes by walk from the old town stands as one of Europe's most captivating showcases of early 20th century architecture. The district's buildings are more than just structures. They are works of art adorned with flowing lines that capture the essense of his artistic movement. Art novel was a popular architectural style across Europe from the late 19th century to the early 20th century. This style is characterized by its flowing lines, intricate details and natural motives. Ria boosts so many art novel buildings thanks to its rapid economic growth in the early 20th century. During this time, the city experienced a building boom and wealthy citizens had large fancy building constructed to show off their wealth. The vast number of art noa buildings in Ria, making up roughly onethird of the city center architecture positions it as one of Europe's most extensive and best preserved collection of this style. Alberta Street is without any doubt the heart of Ria's art no district. This street is lined with some of the most beautiful example of a style featuring elaborate facads adorned with floral motives, intricate carvings and whismical [Music] decorations. On Alberta Street, you'll find the art no museum at number 12. This wellpreserved building offers a glimpse into the era's design, showcasing original furnishing and decor while highlighting the architects who shaped Ria's architectural legacy. The Freedom Monument just outside Ria's old town is a 42 m tall symbol of Latia's independence. Erected in 1935 and funded by public donation, it features a statue of liberty holding free stars representing Latia's historical regions. During the Soviet occupation, it became a clandestine symbol of resistance. A short walk from the monument leads to the Cathedral of Christ nativity. Completed in 1883, this Orthodox Cathedral is renowned for its striking architecture, including golden domes and intricate details. The cathedral features a mix of Byzantine and Russian revival styles with its grand facade and lavish interiors showcasing elaborate frescos and iconography. The Latvia National Museum of Art and architectural gem completed in 1905 is another mustsee. This museum showcases an extensive collection of Latian art from medieval to contemporary works and stands as a key cultural institution in Ria. Next to the museum, you can take some rest at Bast Kala Park, a tranquil green space established in the 19th century. The park offers a picturesque walking path, charming bridge, and scenic views along the canal providing a peaceful retreat from the city's bustel. For a unique perspective of Ria, consider taking a boat tour along the city's canal. These tours offer a fresh view of the city, highlighting its architectural diversity and hidden corners from the [Music] water. The Moscow district of Ria contrasts sharply with the more Polish tourist focused areas of the Latian capital. Its name is coming from a significant Russian speaking community that settled here during the late 19th century. A period of rapid industrialization that transformed the area into a bustling hub for workers and immigrants. A defining feature of the Moscow district is its Soviet era architecture alongside noticeable sign of urban decay. Many buildings display worn facads and dilapidated conditions reflecting the district complex history. The most prominent Soviet landmark is the Latvian Academy of Sciences. Completed in 1961, this 108 m high skyscraper is often compared to the Palace of Culture and Science in Warso. Built in the Stalinist architectural style, it features grand scale classical influences and a strong emphasis on symmetry. In front of the palace, the church of annunciation, built in 1778, is a notable Orthodox church known for its Byzantine architecture and cultural significance. The Lastia cultural quarter serves as a significant cultural and artistic hub. One of its highlight is the giant black fox sculpture, a striking public art piece that embodies the district eclectic and unique character. The Moscow district is also home to the RIA Central Market located on the district [Music] edge. This bustling market housed in former Zeppelin hangers is one of the largest and most diverse in [Music] Europe. As our journey through Ria comes to an end, we have only scratched the surface of the countless wonders this remarkable city holds.
